data.title: Lazard Non-GAAP EPS of $0.42 misses by $0.09, revenue of $673M misses by $40.23M
data.description:
• Lazard press release (LAZ): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.42 misses by $0.09.
  • Revenue of $673M (+4.7% Y/Y) misses by $40.23M.
  • For the first quarter of 2026, Financial Advisory reported net revenue and adjusted net revenue1 of $360 million and $356 million, respectively, 2% and 4% lower than the first quarter of 2025, respectively.

  • Management fees on an adjusted basis1 were $296 million for the first quarter of 2026, 25% higher than the first quarter of 2025, and 3% higher than the fourth quarter of 2025.

    Incentive fees on an adjusted basis1 were $11 million for the first quarter of 2026, compared to $9 million for the first quarter of 2025.

    Other revenue2 on an adjusted basis1 was $1 million for the first quarter of 2026, compared to $18 million for the first quarter of 2025.

    Average assets under management (AUM) was $266 billion for the first quarter of 2026, 15% higher than the first quarter of 2025, and 2% higher than the fourth quarter of 2025.

    AUM as of March 31, 2026 was $259 billion, 14% higher than March 31, 2025, and 2% higher than December 31, 2025. The sequential change from December 31, 2025 was driven by net inflows of $9 billion, market appreciation of $0.4 billion, foreign exchange depreciation of $3 billion, and divestitures of $1.5 billion.

  • Asset Management adjusted net revenue increased 17% year-over-year with positive flows of $9 billion

data.title: Boston Beer narrows 2026 non-GAAP EPS to $8.50-$10.50 while projecting volume down low- to mid-single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Boston Beer Company (SAM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our first quarter depletions were down 4%,” and “Truly remains a meaningful portion of our mix and continues to lose share,” while “Angry Orchard and Dogfish Head have now experienced 4 consecutive quarters of growth,” (Founder, Chairman, President & CEO C. Koch).
  • “Sun Cruiser has quickly grown to Top 5 spirits RTDs and is the fastest-growing brand in the category by volume,” and the company “recently announced a multiyear USGA partnership, making Sun Cruiser the official ready-to-drink cocktail of… the U.S. Open and the U.S. Women’s Open,” (CEO Koch).
  • “We continue to make strong progress on our margin enhancement initiatives, delivering 49.3% first quarter gross margin,” and “we have repurchased over $30 million in shares year-to-date,” (CEO Koch).
  • “We recorded $216 million in total pretax litigation expenses in the quarter… related to a supplier contract dispute,” and “do not expect this issue to have a material impact on our operating plans,” (CFO & Treasurer Diego Reynoso).

Outlook

  • “We are slightly narrowing our 2026 volume range to down low single digits to mid-single digits from our prior guidance of flat to down mid-single digits,” (CEO Koch).
  • “We are narrowing our full year non-GAAP EPS guidance to $8.50 to $10.50 from our prior guidance of $8.50 to $11,” and “we may choose to spend at the lower end of the range depending on the commodity and energy cost environment,” (CFO Reynoso).
  • “We continue to expect full year 2026 reported gross margins to be between 48% and 50%,” and “our 2026 guidance reflects a full year tariff cost estimate of $20 million to $30 million,” (CFO Reynoso).

Financial Results

  • “Revenue for the quarter decreased 4.4%,” while “our first quarter gross margin of 49.3% increased 100 basis points year-over-year,” (CFO Reynoso).
  • “We recorded $216 million in total pretax litigation expenses… [with] a $15.52 impact to our first quarter GAAP EPS,” and “excluding the litigation-related expenses, we reported non-GAAP EPS of $1.64 per diluted share,” (CFO Reynoso).
  • On cost and productivity items, “advertising, promotional and selling expenses… increased $2.5 million,” “general and administrative expenses increased $4.4 million,” and “we reduced obsolete inventories 36% in the first quarter,” (CFO Reynoso).

Q&A

  • Eric Serotta, Morgan Stanley: asked whether Twisted Tea needs a “reset” as declines persist; Founder, Chairman, President & CEO Koch: “I don't think it needs a drastic reset,” citing “the success in the rise of vodka-based teas,” plus “trying to reset some of the pricing… gained shelf space… [and] introduced some new packs.”
  • Gregory Porter, Evercore ISI: asked about Sun Cruiser ACV and regional opportunity; CEO Koch: “It's strongest in New England,” and said “there's definite upside, maybe another 50% ACV,” while noting spirits-distribution and tax differences by state.
  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked why category trends are improving; CEO Koch: “my crystal ball is no better than anybody else's,” but pointed to shifting health dialogue, “a federal ban on hemp-based THC products,” and “less pressure on the Hispanic community,” while adding, “right now, we've actually lost share of what we consider our addressable market.”
  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked about inflation assumptions and savings offsets; CFO Reynoso: “we've got about $12.5 million of inflationary impacts… most of that was aluminum,” and said savings are “a little bit better than we thought… [with] acceleration,” plus a new “revenue management” bucket expected to contribute “in 2027.”
  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: asked about hedging and transport costs; CFO Reynoso: “we do not hedge,” and said diesel/trucking availability is a watch item, with fuel impact “mid-single digits in millions.”
  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: asked about Hard Mountain Dew weakness; CEO Koch: “the hard sodas… have not had the appeal on an enduring basis,” and cited distribution complications where “independent bottlers have been able to block it.”
  • Christopher Barnes, Deutsche Bank: asked about consolidation; CEO Koch: “that's a question to investment bankers,” adding Boston Beer is “big enough to innovate” but “small enough to be nimble,” and “the success of Sun Cruiser… validated our continuing ability to bring new-to-world brands.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing, with pointed framing such as Twisted Tea “stubbornly declining” (Eric Serotta, Morgan Stanley) and detailed cost scrutiny around aluminum/tariffs and inflation (multiple analysts).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks (“I'm encouraged to see modest improvements in category trends,” CEO Koch) but more defensive/guarded in Q&A on brand pressures (“we've actually lost share,” CEO Koch) and costs (“we will update our EPS outlook if commodity inflation continues to increase,” CFO Reynoso).
  • Versus last quarter: management retained the same macro themes but used more constraint language (“dynamic macroeconomic environment,” CEO Koch) and tightened EPS and volume ranges; analyst questioning remained focused on Twisted Tea, inflation/tariffs, and the durability of growth engines.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from “flat to down mid-single digits” volume (Q4 2025) to “down low single digits to mid-single digits” (Q1 2026), and from non-GAAP EPS “$8.50 to $11” (Q4 2025) to “$8.50 to $10.50” (Q1 2026).
  • The call introduced a major one-time P&L item: “$216 million” pretax litigation expense (Q1 2026), which was not discussed as an expense line item in the prior quarter prepared remarks.
  • Management continued emphasizing margin initiatives and Beyond Beer, while Q1 2026 added more explicit near-term cost monitoring around “additional inflation in energy and aluminum” (CFO Reynoso).

Risks and Concerns

  • “We continue to anticipate volume headwinds for 2026,” citing “a dynamic macroeconomic environment and evolving geopolitical developments,” (CEO Koch).
  • Cost exposure centered on “additional inflation in energy and aluminum,” plus tariffs, with the company stating, “We do not hedge commodities,” (CFO Reynoso).
  • Legal exposure remained a key uncertainty: “We cannot estimate when or if damages or interest will ultimately be paid,” (CFO Reynoso).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter where depletions declined and portfolio performance remained mixed, with Sun Cruiser and margin execution offset by ongoing pressure in Truly and softness in parts of the core. The company tightened full-year volume and non-GAAP EPS ranges, citing updated volume trends and higher expected energy/aluminum inflation, while reiterating gross margin expectations and flexibility in brand spending. A large supplier-contract litigation charge dominated GAAP results, with management stating it does not expect the dispute to change operating plans.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AM Markets Need to Know: Meta and AI, Trump's naval blockade, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures were muted as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note.

Now, here are 5 news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

Meta shifts spending toward AI, signals more layoffs possible: Meta (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the company’s planned job cuts stem from increased capital spending for artificial intelligence and did not rule out further layoffs, Reuters reported. “We basically have two major cost centers in the company: compute infrastructure and people-oriented things,” he said during a town hall on Thursday. “If we’re investing more in one area to serve our community, then that means we have less capital to allocate to the other.”

Blue Owl crystalizes tenfold return on SpaceX stake: Blue Owl Capital (OWL) co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said the company has sold about half of its stake in SpaceX (SPACE) at a $1.25T valuation, realizing roughly a tenfold return. “We made about 10x our money,” Lipschultz said on an earnings call, noting the firm still holds about half of its position. Blue Owl was among SpaceX’s earliest lenders and later converted its exposure into an equity investment.

Trump maintains Iran naval blockade amid oil price surge: The U.S. and Iran continue to lock horns in a prolonged standoff, with President Donald Trump reaffirming his commitment to a naval blockade of Iranian ports despite surging oil prices and global economic concerns. Trump said the blockade is effectively “choking” Iran’s economy and signaled it would remain in place until Tehran agrees to curb its nuclear program. “Their economy is crashing; the blockade is incredible,” Trump told reporters at the White House.

Trump signs executive order to expand retirement savings access: President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Thursday to create a government website allowing people in the U.S. to find and compare private-sector retirement savings plans. The aim is to help more Americans sign on for retirement plans before next year, when the federal government will start matching retirement contributions made by low- and middle-income workers.

Trump grants permit reviving portions of Keystone XL pipeline: President Trump signed an order Thursday granting a cross-border permit to the Bridger pipeline expansion project that would revive parts of the Keystone XL pipeline to transport Canadian oil to Guernsey, Wyoming. The pipeline, proposed by South Bow (SOBO) and U.S. partner Bridger Pipeline, would travel 647 miles through eastern Montana and Wyoming, carrying 550K bbl/day of Canadian crude and potentially increasing Canada’s exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.

data.title: Apple CEO flags rising memory costs as ongoing headwind
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook flagged rising memory prices, which are expected to weigh more heavily on the company’s costs in coming quarters.

Speaking on the company’s earnings call on Thursday, Cook said memory costs are set to be "significantly higher" in the June quarter and will have a larger effect beyond that.

Memory costs had a "minimal impact" in the December quarter but rose in the March period, partly offset by carry-in inventory benefits, Apple said. 

"Beyond the June quarter, we believe memory costs will drive an increasing impact on our business," Cook said.

"We'll continue to evaluate this, we'll look at a range of options"

Apple on Thursday reported strong second-quarter earnings driven by strong demand for the iPhone 17. Per-share profit was $2.01, beating estimates by $0.07, on revenue of $111.18B, which grew 16.5%, also ahead of expectations.

Q1 gross margin of 49.3% did include the impact of tariff-related costs. Cook also said tariffs had a smaller impact in the March quarter compared with the prior period due to lower product volumes and reduced rates, adding the company would reinvest any tariff refunds into U.S. innovation and manufacturing.Shares are up 3.2% in premarket trading on Friday.

data.title: Bio-Rad outlines 2026 currency-neutral revenue growth of -3% to +0.5% amid Middle East disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jonathan DiVincenzo (President & COO) said Bio-Rad “reported Q1 results within our revenue guidance” while facing “several external pressures, most notably associated with the ongoing conflict in the Middle East,” adding that in 2025 the region “represented over 9% of our Diagnostics segment, primarily driven by our blood typing franchise,” and that the conflict “will be a significant headwind for revenue and margin for full year 2026.”

  • DiVincenzo said Life Science “reported net sales were flat,” with “academic demand…constrained, particularly in the Americas,” and noted Bio-Rad’s customer checks suggest “there continues to be considerable disruption,” plus “a lag between funding approvals and purchasing activity.” He added, “In biopharma, we are seeing early signs of stabilization,” with “later-stage companies…more robust,” and Bio-Rad expects “gradual improvement through the year.”

  • DiVincenzo highlighted digital PCR momentum: “ddPCR instrument revenue grew 24% over prior year,” and said the QX700 platform is “driving both competitive wins and conversion from qPCR,” with “over 99% of our digital PCR assays…available on the new QX700 series.”

  • Roop Lakkaraju (Executive VP & CFO) reported “net sales…approximately $592 million,” and said Bio-Rad’s non-GAAP results were “$51 million or $1.89 diluted earnings per share,” while the Sartorius fair-value change “contributed $562 million to our reported net loss of $527 million or $19.55 per diluted share.”

  • Norman Schwartz (Chairman & CEO) said Bio-Rad’s M&A posture has evolved: “our focus has shifted from early-stage opportunities to companies with demonstrated revenue and margin profiles,” targeting “companies within the $100 million to $500 million revenue range,” adding, “We’re not, at the moment, focused on anything transformative.”

Outlook

  • Lakkaraju said, “We have decided to adjust our 2026 guidance,” citing Middle East demand softness and logistics, and guided full-year currency-neutral revenue growth “between minus 3% and plus 0.5%,” with Life Science currency-neutral growth “between minus 3% and minus 1%” and Diagnostics “between minus 3% and plus 1%.”

  • Lakkaraju projected “full year non-GAAP gross margin…between 53% and 54%” and “full year non-GAAP operating margin…between 10% and 12%,” and said Bio-Rad updated full-year free cash flow to “approximately $290 million to $340 million.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance, Bio-Rad moved from currency-neutral revenue growth of “between 0.5% and 1.5%” and non-GAAP operating margin of “between 12% and 12.5%” to the updated ranges Lakkaraju provided in Q1.

Financial Results

  • No reliable analyst estimates comparison was included because the provided estimates data does not use the transcript-required quarter format (Q1–Q4).

  • Lakkaraju said Life Science segment sales were “$229 million,” and Diagnostics segment sales were “approximately $364 million,” adding that the Middle East conflict “resulting in an $11 million impact to the business in the quarter” within Diagnostics.

  • Lakkaraju reported consolidated gross margin was “52.3%,” with non-GAAP gross margin “53.1% versus 53.8%,” and attributed the year-over-year pressure to factors including “unfavorable manufacturing absorption…40 basis points,” “higher instruments versus consumables mix…30 basis points,” “higher freight fuel surcharges…20 basis points” and “FX…20 basis points.”

  • Lakkaraju said free cash flow was “$78 million,” and that Bio-Rad repurchased “176,000 shares…at a total cost of approximately $48 million,” with “approximately $237 million available for additional buybacks.”

Q&A

  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research LLC: asked why Middle East impact was “more prominent for Bio-Rad” and how it affected logistics; Jonathan DiVincenzo (President & COO) said Bio-Rad’s exposure is tied to tender wins and scale, noting the region reached “9% of the Diagnostics business,” and that Bio-Rad “expected solid high double-digit growth in that region” before the slowdown.

  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research LLC: asked about cost actions to protect earnings and reach “mid-teens operating margins”; Roop Lakkaraju (Executive VP & CFO) said Bio-Rad has “begun to tamp down discretionary spend” and is evaluating “structural things…in how we run the business,” but said it was “a little bit early” to provide specifics.

  • Brandon Couillard, Wells Fargo: asked about Q2/Q3 phasing; Lakkaraju said Bio-Rad expects “about a 5% lift from Q1 to Q2,” then “slightly into Q3,” with Q4 as “our seasonally strongest quarter,” citing quality controls batch releases and blood typing seasonality outside the Middle East.

  • Brandon Couillard, Wells Fargo: asked about ddPCR consumables softness and competitive dynamics; DiVincenzo said consumables were “soft in the quarter” across “academic and even…biopharma,” emphasized the “equation here is growing our installed base,” and added, “we are taking share from qPCR,” while Lakkaraju confirmed the consumables decline characterization: “you’re spot on in terms of low double digits.”

  • Daniel Leonard, RBC: asked whether margin offsets are being taken given “pretty severe” decrementals; Lakkaraju said Bio-Rad has “near-term actions…in process” and on broader actions said, “stay tuned,” while DiVincenzo added Bio-Rad is assessing whether there are “appropriate surcharges or ways to mitigate some of the additional costs.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative, pressing on the magnitude of the Middle East impact and decremental margins, including Daniel Leonard’s focus on “pretty severe” decrementals and Jack Meehan’s questions on how Bio-Rad will “protect and grow earnings.”

  • Management tone was slightly cautious but solution-oriented, repeatedly stressing controllables and evaluation of actions, including Lakkaraju’s “without getting into too many specifics at this time” and “stay tuned,” alongside DiVincenzo’s “we remain confident these actions will translate into improved financial performance over time.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more constructive framing around “early signs of stabilization” (Norman Schwartz, Q4 2025) and expectations that gross margin pressures were “execution-related rather than structural” (Jonathan DiVincenzo, Q4 2025), Q1 2026 carried more explicit disruption language tied to the Middle East and corresponding guidance reduction.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The most material shift was guidance: Bio-Rad moved from Q4’s 2026 currency-neutral revenue growth outlook of “between 0.5% and 1.5%” (Roop Lakkaraju, Q4 2025) to Q1’s updated “between minus 3% and plus 0.5%” (Roop Lakkaraju, Q1 2026), explicitly attributing the change to the Middle East conflict.

  • Strategic emphasis broadened from Q4’s focus on supply chain execution and QX700 launch traction to Q1’s added operational localization and tariff mitigation actions; DiVincenzo said Bio-Rad “began manufacture of select life science instruments in China for China,” to support tenders while “minimizing tariff exposure.”

  • Analysts’ questions rotated from Q4’s ddPCR growth and process chromatography recovery timeline to Q1’s concentration on geopolitical exposure, phasing, and what actions management will take to offset margin and freight/logistics pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • DiVincenzo warned the Middle East conflict “will be a significant headwind for revenue and margin for full year 2026,” and Lakkaraju said the disruption created “continued demand softness” and “challenges getting product to our channel partners and into end customers.”

  • Management cited academic funding friction as a continued Life Science constraint; DiVincenzo said there is “considerable disruption” and “a lag between funding approvals and purchasing activity,” while Lakkaraju attributed weakness to “ongoing challenges in the academic research market, particularly in the Americas.”

  • Freight and supply chain costs remained a near-term margin risk; Lakkaraju pointed to “higher freight fuel surcharges,” and in Q&A said Q2 gross margin is expected to “tick down just a tad…specific to freight.”

Final Takeaway

Bio-Rad management framed Q1 2026 execution as on-plan versus its revenue guidance but dominated by an unexpected and material Middle East disruption in Diagnostics that also pressured margins via manufacturing absorption and logistics costs. Management updated full-year 2026 guidance ranges across revenue growth, margins, and free cash flow to reflect ongoing conflict impacts, while reiterating priorities around ddPCR installed base expansion led by QX700, operational efficiency actions, and a more targeted M&A approach focused on proven businesses in the $100 million to $500 million revenue range.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
